facts.fact: PostgreSQL default shared_buffers is 128MB, but industry recommendation is 25% of total system RAM (e.g., 8GB on a 32GB system)
WAL buffer (wal_buffers) auto-calculates to 1/32nd of shared_buffers (minimum 64kB, maximum 16MB) when set to -1
Checkpoint timeout defaults to 5 minutes; max_wal_size defaults to 1GB (soft limit, can be exceeded under heavy load)
full_page_writes (enabled by default) writes entire 8KB data pages to WAL on first modification after checkpoint to ensure crash recovery from partially written pages
PostgreSQL uses Snapshot Isolation for REPEATABLE READ, preventing all anomalies except serialization anomalies; true SERIALIZABLE uses Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) with predicate locking
READ COMMITTED (default isolation level) allows nonrepeatable reads and phantom reads; each SQL command sees a new snapshot

Long-running transactions block VACUUM from reclaiming dead tuples generated by UPDATE/DELETE operations that occurred after the transaction started, regardless of whether the transaction is active or idle
Autovacuum vacuum threshold = autovacuum_vacuum_threshold (default 50) + autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor (default 20%) × reltuples; similar formula for analyze and insert vacuums
Tables must be vacuumed at least once every 2 billion transactions to prevent transaction ID wraparound data loss
B-tree is PostgreSQL's default index type; handles equality and range queries; upper tree levels often cached in memory
Hash indexes can only handle simple equality comparisons and are often no faster than B-tree for equality queries due to memory caching advantages of B-tree's tree structure
GiST indexes are lossy (may produce false matches) and are balanced tree structures where internal nodes represent 'bounding boxes' enclosing child regions; commonly used for spatial data via PostGIS
pg_blocking_pids() function returns the process IDs of sessions blocking a given PID but can be resource-intensive on high-concurrency systems and should be used judiciously in production
pg_stat_activity.wait_event_type and wait_event fields diagnose blocking and I/O issues; one row per server process showing running query details
PostgreSQL uses Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC); deleted and updated rows are marked dead but remain in storage until vacuumed, allowing concurrent readers to see old versions
WAL writer process flushes WAL every wal_writer_delay (default 200ms) or when wal_writer_flush_after (default 1MB) is reached

synchronous_commit modes: 'remote_apply' (standby durable to OS crash), 'on' (standby durable to PG crash), 'remote_write' (standby receives WAL but not durably), 'local' (only local durable), 'off' (asynchronous)
checkpoint_completion_target (default 0.9) spreads checkpoint I/O across 90% of the checkpoint interval to reduce I/O spikes
pgbench benchmarks show 14% TPS improvement when increasing shared_buffers from default 128MB to 1GB, and 53% cumulative improvement by 32GB allocation (on systems with 64GB RAM)
Recovery with recovery_prefetch = 'try' prefetches blocks referenced in WAL during recovery to reduce I/O wait times; wal_decode_buffer_size (default 512kB) limits prefetch lookahead distance
SERIALIZABLE isolation level in PostgreSQL uses Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) with predicate locking (no explicit locks), detecting read/write conflicts to prevent all anomalies

examples.title: Netflix: Handling Long-Running Transactions in High-Throughput Systems
examples.description: Netflix runs massive ETL pipelines that hold transactions open for hours to maintain snapshot consistency across analytical queries. Long-running transactions prevent autovacuum from reclaiming dead tuples, causing table bloat to grow faster than vacuum can clean. Netflix mitigated this by implementing per-table autovacuum tuning: reducing autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor from 20% to 2-5% on high-churn fact tables and running manual VACUUM during off-peak hours.
examples.metric: Reduced table bloat from 40% to <5% on fact tables with 100M+ rows
examples.context: Analytics workload with batch ingestion every 6 hours

examples.title: Stripe: WAL Compression for Replication Lag
examples.description: Stripe uses synchronous_commit = 'remote_apply' for critical payment tables to ensure standby replicas see all committed transactions before acknowledging writes to the client. With high write throughput (10k+ TPS), full_page_writes at every checkpoint (5 min default) generated 15GB of WAL per hour. Enabling wal_compression with zstd reduced WAL volume by 40% without increasing recovery time, because compression overhead is negligible on modern CPUs.
examples.context: Critical financial ledger table with 100M transactions/day

examples.title: Databricks: Index Selection for Mixed Workloads
examples.description: Databricks' lakehouse metadata service switched from B-tree to GiST indexes for partition pruning queries on spatial-like path hierarchies (e.g., /bucket/folder/table/partition). GiST's bounding-box model reduced index scan time from 45ms to 8ms because false matches were rare and single-page lookups dominated the cost. However, hash indexes were rejected because partition pruning requires range predicates (e.g., path LIKE '/bucket/%'), which hash indexes cannot support.

examples.title: Slack: Isolation Level Trade-offs in User Data Writes
examples.description: Slack's messaging platform initially used READ COMMITTED for user data updates (messages, reactions) to minimize latency, but encountered race conditions where two users could remove the same reaction concurrently, leaving inconsistent state. Switching to REPEATABLE READ forced 0.5-2% of transactions to retry on serialization conflicts, but eliminated the anomaly. They reserved SERIALIZABLE for financial (billing) operations and rare admin tasks where the strictness justified the cost.
examples.metric: Serialization retry rate: 0.8% under peak load; latency increase <5ms median

examples.title: Uber: Crash Recovery Tuning for High-Availability Checkpoints
examples.description: Uber runs checkpoint_timeout = 5min and max_wal_size = 1GB in production, but tested reducing max_wal_size to 256MB on mission-critical regional shards to cut crash recovery time from 10 minutes to 2 minutes. The trade-off: checkpoints triggered by WAL size grew from ~1/hour to ~3/hour, increasing background write I/O by 25%. For non-critical analytical databases, they kept the defaults and accepted longer recovery windows.
examples.context: Regional database shard with 50k QPS and 500GB working set

analogies.analogy: Buffer Pool as a Retail Store's Stockroom
analogies.explanation: The buffer pool (shared_buffers) is like a store's back-room inventory. Frequently sold items stay on the shelf (hot pages cached in RAM), while slow-moving stock requires a trip to the warehouse (cold pages fetched from disk). If the stockroom is too small (low shared_buffers), the store constantly restocks from the warehouse, wasting employee time. The optimal size balances inventory holding costs (RAM) against restocking labor (disk I/O).

analogies.analogy: WAL as a Ship's Log (Write-Ahead Logging)
analogies.explanation: Write-Ahead Logging is like a ship's log: before modifying cargo (data), the captain records the change in the log (WAL) and ensures the log survives (fsync to disk). If the ship crashes, the log reveals what happened and which transactions were committed vs. partial. full_page_writes are like taking a full inventory snapshot at checkpoints—more expensive to record but guarantees fast recovery if the manifest was torn during a power failure.

analogies.analogy: MVCC as Multiple Newspapers on a Newsstand
analogies.explanation: MVCC is like a newsstand with multiple editions of a newspaper in stock. When an update happens, the old edition isn't thrown away—it stays until all readers of that edition finish. Readers see the edition from when they started reading (snapshot isolation), and new readers see the latest edition. Vacuum is the cleanup staff that removes old editions after all readers are done with them. Long-running transactions are like customers holding a 3-day-old newspaper—the cleanup staff can't remove it, so it piles up.

analogies.analogy: Isolation Levels as Bathroom Occupancy Policies
analogies.explanation: READ COMMITTED is like a bathroom where each person sees a new snapshot (mirrors show current state). REPEATABLE READ is like a bathroom where your mirror reflects the moment you entered—you see a consistent view, but someone else might be changing things you don't notice. SERIALIZABLE is like turning the bathroom into a single-occupancy room—only one person at a time, guaranteed consistency, but slower throughput.

analogies.analogy: Index Types as Lookup Methods in an Encyclopedia
analogies.explanation: B-tree indexes are like an alphabetical index at the back of an encyclopedia—fast for both exact matches ('Q' words) and ranges ('Q-R' words). Hash indexes are like a deck of flashcards organized by hash value—only fast if you know the exact card you want. GiST indexes are like organizing books on a library floor by region (one shelf covers Alaska, another California) where you might fetch books from adjacent regions by mistake before narrowing down your search.

key_numbers.context: Too small for production; industry standard is 25% of system RAM
e.g., 8GB on 32GB system; pgbench shows 53% TPS improvement at 32GB allocation vs. 128MB default
On busy servers, increasing to a few MB can improve write performance
Longer timeouts increase crash recovery time; shorter timeouts increase background I/O
Soft limit that can be exceeded under heavy load; affects crash recovery time
Ensures WAL space for workload spikes and enables segment recycling
WAL writer flushes after this delay or when wal_writer_flush_after (default 1MB) is reached
Triggers flush when 1MB of WAL data is accumulated
Multiplier applied to table size for vacuum threshold
Tables must be vacuumed at least once per 2B transactions to prevent data loss
Spreads checkpoint I/O across 90% of checkpoint interval
Triggers forced vacuum to prevent transaction ID wraparound
Entire data page written to WAL on first modification after checkpoint
Limits prefetch lookahead during crash recovery
Each 8GB step provides ~30% additional improvement at high allocation levels

key_numbers.metric: Full page writes WAL volume overhead

key_numbers.context: Ensures local durability; standby durability to PostgreSQL crash (not OS crash)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Increasing shared_buffers to 50% or more of system RAM improves performance proportionally
common_misconceptions.reality: 25% is the industry standard; beyond that, diminishing returns kick in because the OS needs buffer cache, connection overhead grows, and cache coherency costs increase. Allocating >50% often degrades performance.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Disabling full_page_writes improves performance without risk if you have a battery-backed write cache
common_misconceptions.reality: full_page_writes protects against torn pages during OS/hardware crashes, not just database crashes. Battery-backed cache only protects against power failure, not SSD firmware bugs or kernel panics that may cause partial writes. Disabling is risky unless you have hardware that guarantees atomic page writes.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Long-running idle transactions don't cause problems—they're not active, so they don't hold locks
common_misconceptions.reality: Idle transactions block VACUUM from reclaiming dead tuples across the entire table. A single idle `BEGIN` statement open for hours can cause bloat to accumulate at the rate of your write traffic, filling up the table and slowing down scans.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash indexes are always faster than B-tree for exact-match lookups
common_misconceptions.reality: B-tree upper levels are often cached in memory and leverage CPU caches well. Hash indexes incur hash computation overhead and random I/O patterns that break cache locality. B-tree is often equally fast or faster for equality queries, plus it supports range queries and is the default.

common_misconceptions.misconception: REPEATABLE READ prevents all isolation anomalies
common_misconceptions.reality: REPEATABLE READ prevents nonrepeatable reads and phantom reads (in PostgreSQL's snapshot isolation implementation) but allows serialization anomalies—write skew and other conflicts between concurrent transactions. SERIALIZABLE is needed for full isolation.

common_misconceptions.misconception: GiST indexes are too slow because they're lossy and require heap lookups to filter false positives
common_misconceptions.reality: False positive rate depends on data distribution and index design. For spatial data (PostGIS), false positive rates are often <5%, and the speed advantage of GiST's bounding-box pruning outweighs heap lookup costs compared to B-tree scans.

common_misconceptions.misconception: pg_blocking_pids() tells you which queries are slow
common_misconceptions.reality: pg_blocking_pids() only shows lock dependencies—which session is holding a lock that another session is waiting for. A slow query that's not blocking anyone won't appear. Use wait_event and query_start time for slow queries.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Autovacuum should be disabled on high-traffic tables in favor of manual vacuum
common_misconceptions.reality: Autovacuum is essential for transaction ID wraparound prevention and dead tuple cleanup. Disabling it risks data loss and bloat. Tuning autovacuum parameters (scale factors, cost delays) is the solution, not disabling it.

Shorter checkpoints reduce recovery time but increase background I/O during normal operation. Full_page_writes already enable fast recovery; the trade-off is between checkpoint frequency (background I/O cost) and recovery time (crash cost).

common_misconceptions.misconception: SERIALIZABLE isolation level uses explicit row locks like FOR UPDATE
common_misconceptions.reality: SERIALIZABLE in PostgreSQL uses Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) with predicate locking—no explicit locks. Conflicts are detected by tracking read/write dependencies, and conflicts cause serialization errors (not blocking). SELECT FOR UPDATE is needed only for explicit pessimistic locking.
